title: Estimated Warranty Liability
event: Reyes Co. sells $500,000 of products in December and estimates warranty costs at 2% of sales. In January, it replaces defective parts costing $1,400 under warranty. Prepare both entries.

lines.label: 500,000 × 2%
lines.why: Matching demands the expense in the SALE's period — the promise was part of the sale.
lines.trap: Waiting for actual claims pushes the expense into the wrong year.

lines.label: future claims reserved
lines.why: A probable, estimable obligation — the definition of an accruable contingency.
lines.trap: Estimated, but still a real liability on the balance sheet.

lines.label: claim honored
lines.why: Actual repairs SPEND the liability, not the income statement.
lines.trap: No Warranty Expense here — that would double-count what the estimate already recorded.

lines.label: parts used
lines.why: The parts leave inventory to fix the product.
lines.trap: Credit whatever resource was consumed — parts, cash, or wages payable.

memorize.body: Same architecture as bad debts: estimate the expense with the revenue, then let actual events consume the reserve.
Accrue a contingency only if it's PROBABLE and ESTIMABLE — reasonably possible ones are disclosed, remote ones ignored.

questions.prompt: Warranty EXPENSE recorded in January when the $1,400 repair is made = ?

distractors.feedback: The expense hit December, with the sale. January's repair consumes the liability already on the books.

build.scaffold: Sale period: expense the estimate. Claim period: debit the liability, credit the resource used.
